rationale: SALLM delivers a reproducible security benchmark—dataset, configurable assessment, and novel secure-generation metrics—that an org can run to measure AI code risk; passes operationalizability squarely under Tier 1 AI code risk/quality.

Builds a live-programming editor and runs a controlled study showing reduced over/under-reliance on AI code, a concrete oversight mechanism for developer validation of LLM suggestions; direct Tier 1 hit.

Position paper but proposes a concrete four-function verification side-car (autoformalize, validate, feedback, developer-steerable specs) that is an actionable oversight mechanism for vibe-coding risk; the actionable architecture outweighs its position-piece framing.
Empirically benchmarks GPT-3.5/4 vulnerability-detection performance across prompts, yielding an actionable detection signal for security review; Tier 1 AI code risk with reproducible method.

Quantifies insecure-generation rates (up to 65%) across four LLMs using the CWE catalogue and shows guidance-driven mitigation to near-100% secure, a measured methodology with direct oversight implication.

Large-scale measurement (576k samples, 16 LLMs) of package-hallucination rates plus tested mitigations directly informs supply-chain risk management of AI-generated code; strong Tier 1 quantification.
Introduces PrimeVul and rigorous evaluation exposing that code-LM vulnerability detection is drastically overestimated, a reproducible reliability signal for orgs deploying detection tools.
Systematic grey-literature review of 518 firsthand accounts characterizing vibe-coding QA practices and the speed-quality paradox sits at the core domain anchor with team-level implications.

Provides a calibrated probabilistic estimate of generated-program correctness with human-preferred predicates, an operationalizable trust-calibration signal for validating LLM code; org-governance framing is absent but the mechanism transfers directly.
Presents a systematic black-box inversion method that automatically surfaces thousands of security vulnerabilities in code-gen models including Copilot, a reproducible risk-assessment mechanism.

Causal difference-in-differences study quantifying Cursor's transient velocity gain versus persistent rise in complexity and static-analysis warnings identifies QA as a scaling bottleneck—actionable measurement at the org level.

SecureVibeBench benchmarks code agents on realistic multi-file secure-coding tasks with static+dynamic oracles, showing only 23.8% correct-and-secure; a reproducible risk metric for agent-generated code.
SecRepoBench evaluates secure code completion across 27 real-world repos and 15 CWEs over 29 LLMs and 15 agents, a reproducible benchmark directly measuring AI code security risk.
Proposes VIBE4M, a framework of workflows, policies, and practices to manage maintainability risk of vibe-coded software, an org-level governance mechanism despite its review/position framing.

Directly addresses the reviewer-capacity bottleneck from AI code volume with a five-stage agentic code-review workflow retaining human quality gates—core Tier 1 code-review scalability with oversight angle.

Proposes the SAFE-AI governance framework plus a suggestive/generative/autonomous/destructive taxonomy with guardrails and human-in-the-loop controls, an actionable org-governance contribution aligned to EU AI Act.

Studies verification-trace design for human oversight of agentic AI and empirically tests interfaces that speed error-finding; the oversight/verification mechanism transfers to reviewing agent-generated code without substantial re-derivation.

Empirically strong but targets vulnerability/maintainability of MCP server tool infrastructure and ecosystem governance, not oversight or risk measurement of AI-generated code; mapping to the domain anchor of scaling human review of AI code requires substantial re-derivation.
Quantifies vulnerabilities across 2,500 GPT-4-generated PHP sites with a reproducible hybrid scanning methodology and public artifacts, a benchmarkable measurement of AI code risk.

Defines a five-level agent-autonomy framework (operator to observer) with certification ideas that transfers directly to calibrating oversight of coding agents; the autonomy-control mechanism generalizes to code governance.

CodeSecEval quantifies LLM-generated code vulnerabilities across 44 types with 180 samples and proposes concrete mitigation strategies; a directly usable benchmark and detection signal for AI code risk. Clear Tier 1 direct hit on reproducible risk measurement.

Quantifies AI-generated code quality via a subsequent-rework/deletion metric that an org could adopt as a quality signal for AI vs human output; the productivity framing does not negate the measurable quality-decline finding. Tier 1 AI code quality with reproducible method.
Reproducible empirical evaluation of ChatGPT code security plus prompt-based mitigation gives an actionable detection-and-remediation method. Tier 1 AI code risk quantified with methodology.

First grounded-theory study of vibe coding that surfaces concrete governance-relevant risks (code review burden, trust-regulated delegation continuum) and shared mitigation practices. Directly on the domain anchor with actionable risk findings.

Carllm is a fine-tuned LLM mechanism that improves both accuracy and human-comprehensibility of automated code review comments, directly supporting reviewer decision-making at scale. Tier 1 code review capacity hit.

The empirically derived PICSE framework operationalizes the factors driving trust calibration in AI-assisted software tools, directly usable for oversight of code generators. Tier 1 developer trust/oversight.

Comparative benchmarking of Copilot, CodeWhisperer, and ChatGPT across validity, correctness, security, and technical-debt metrics gives reproducible tool-selection and risk signals. Tier 1 AI code quality quantified.

Signal Detection Theory framework for effective human oversight defines sensitivity/response-bias levers for error detection that transfer to reviewing AI code outputs with trivial re-derivation. Tier 2 human oversight mechanism that generalizes.

Presents a bias-testing framework quantifying fairness defects in LLM code and validates a concrete feedback-driven mitigation (59.88% to 4.79%). Tier 1 AI code risk with reproducible measurement and mechanism.

Systematic review of automation-bias frequency, mediators, and mitigators (training, accountability, DSS design) offers actionable interventions against reviewer over-reliance on AI output; healthcare domain but mitigators transfer directly. Tier 2 oversight mechanism generalizes.

CodeReviewer is a pre-trained model automating change-quality estimation, comment generation, and refinement with a benchmark dataset—directly addresses scaling review to AI code volume. Tier 1 code review automation.

Assured LLMSE proposes a generate-and-test framework with semantic filters guaranteeing non-regression and measurable improvement while positioning the human as final reviewer—an operationalizable oversight-scaling mechanism despite the brief keynote format. Tier 1/2 oversight process.

Empirically benchmarks seven LLMs against static analysis for security code review and identifies prompt and code factors driving performance, giving actionable detection guidance. Tier 1 security review with measured mechanisms.

Quantifies the shift of rework and technical-debt burden onto core developers (6.5% more review, 19% productivity drop) after Copilot adoption—directly measures the oversight-scaling tension central to this SLR. Tier 1 review capacity/fatigue.

Empirically quantifies APR susceptibility to adversarial bug reports (90% attacker-aligned) and measures pre/post-repair defenses including human oversight, providing a threat model and testing framework for AI-fix risk. Tier 1 AI code risk quantified.

Proposes an adversarial-attack classification and robustness-testing approach for code LLMs, an operationalizable testing mechanism for AI code risk, but the truncated abstract lacks quantified results so a human should verify substance. Tier 1 on topic but thin evidence.

ESBMC-AI integrates LLMs with bounded model checking for automated vulnerability detection/repair, validated on 50,000 C programs with CI/CD integration—a concrete verifiable quality-assurance mechanism. Tier 1 AI code risk operationalized.

Empirically characterizes auto-merged agentic PRs versus human ones (size, maintainer caution, tool-level auto-merge rates), directly informing repository policy on when AI-code review can safely be skipped. Tier 1 oversight/governance at scale.

ZORO turns passive rules files into active, enforced, evolvable controls that make coding agents provably follow rules—a directly adoptable human-agent alignment governance mechanism for vibe coding. Tier 1 direct hit with evaluated mechanism.

Empirically shows high trust causes users to miss automated-system errors and that speed/self-correction shape trust—findings that transfer to reviewer over-trust of AI code without substantial re-derivation. Tier 2 automation-trust mechanism generalizes to code oversight.

Proposes four operationalizable interaction dimensions (task alignment, verifiability, steerability, adaptability) that directly structure human oversight of coding agents; the framework is a concrete lens a team can adopt for reviewing AI code.

This is meta-methodology for designing trust experiments, not an oversight mechanism or measurement a team can act on; applying it to code trust requires building new instruments, so it fails the operationalizability test.

Quantifies LLM vulnerability detection gains via a reproducible graph-plus-in-context-learning method, providing a concrete detection signal deployable in code review; the FOR argument on actionable measurement beats the narrow-scope objection.

Title is a direct hit on taming AI-generated code for production (vibe coding governance), but no abstract exists to confirm operationalizability, so a human should verify the content before finalizing.

Case study catalogs concrete LLM code-generation failure modes (hallucination, inconsistency) and human-oversight mitigations (structured prompting, automated testing, iterative refinement); single-project generalizability is a weakness but the actionable lessons win.

Canonical automation-bias study characterizing omission/commission errors under imperfect automated aids; the mechanism transfers directly to reviewers over-trusting AI code suggestions without domain re-derivation.
Interview study surfacing five trust challenges and seven reliability solutions for ChatGPT-generated code; directly on developer oversight and trust calibration of AI code.

Survey and interviews of 574 developers on GenAI code licensing/copyright with regulator-facing recommendations; code-specific legal-liability content directly serves org governance of AI coding tools.

Quantifies LLM vulnerability identification, discovery, and patching via a reproducible chain-of-thought prompting approach; an operationalizable detection and repair signal for AI code risk despite failure-case gaps.

Benchmarks LLMs as vulnerability detectors with reproducible zero/few-shot accuracy metrics across languages, giving a detection signal usable in review pipelines; consistent with keeping sibling vuln-detection papers, transferability holds.

Empirically quantifies how targeted data poisoning induces AI code generators to emit hard-to-detect vulnerable code and offers mitigation insight; directly actionable for managing AI code supply-chain risk.

Experimentally validated cognitive-forcing interventions reduce AI overreliance, a mechanism that transfers to countering reviewers rubber-stamping AI-generated code; domain-general study but the oversight mechanism generalizes cleanly.

Empirical study on predicting code-review comment locations across hundreds of thousands of changesets provides a concrete mechanism and research agenda for scaling review capacity to high AI-generated volume.
Demonstrates covert poisoning attacks (Covert, TrojanPuzzle) on code-suggestion models with explicit practitioner implications for vetting training data; directly actionable AI code risk governance.
FoSE vision/roadmap for trustworthy AI4SE is governance-relevant but aspirational, offering no concrete measurable mechanism, metric, or process a team can adopt; fails the operationalizability test.
Large-scale empirical dataset (456k coding-agent PRs) revealing a trust/acceptance gap and complexity differences; directly informs org-level oversight of autonomous coding agents at scale.

Empirically quantifies security weaknesses by CWE in Copilot/CodeWhisperer/Codeium code merged into real projects and measures a chat-based fix rate; a reproducible risk-measurement and mitigation methodology.
SecCodePLT provides a scalable benchmark with dynamic metrics for evaluating security risk and capability of code-generating LLMs across 44 CWE categories; a reproducible evaluation tool for orgs.

Lessons on deploying static analysis across hundreds of millions of lines are a transferable scaling-oversight mechanism for high-volume code quality checks, but the thin abstract and absent AI-generation angle warrant human verification.

Quantifies human-effort reduction in peer review via automated static analysis plus reviewer recommendation (93% comment-fix agreement, 60-92% recommender accuracy); a concrete mechanism for scaling review capacity that transfers to AI-code volume.

Provides a reproducible automated benchmark quantifying LLM coding assistants' propensity to generate insecure code, a direct org-level risk-measurement tool. Tier-1 direct hit on AI code risk quantification with methodology.

Field study of how professional developers retain control and agency over AI coding agents surfaces concrete oversight strategies and task-suitability guidance. Directly on-target for developer oversight of AI-generated code.

Identifies specific UI oversight mechanisms (confidence highlighting, alternate translations) that help engineers detect and correct imperfect AI-generated code. Operationalizable human-review support for AI code, so keep wins over the interview-only limitation.
Empirically analyzes 229 real developer reactions to ChatGPT code-review comments, yielding actionable insight on LLM-assisted review effectiveness. Tier-1 hit on AI in code review.

Industry interview study yields the PICSE trust framework applicable to AI-based developer tools, an actionable governance/trust lens. Generality beyond AI is a slight weakness but the framework explicitly covers AI tools, so keep.

Delivers a repository-level hallucination taxonomy plus a validated RAG-based mitigation for LLM code generation, a concrete risk-reduction mechanism. Actionable detection-and-mitigation outweighs the light org-governance framing.

Presents a reproducible LLM-powered test-generation technique validated specifically on AI-generated plausible programs with strong measured gains. Concrete QA detection mechanism for AI-code defects.

Offers a parameter-efficient LLM code-review automation framework that can scale reviewer throughput, matching the code-review-capacity criterion. Lack of explicit AI-generated-code-volume framing is a minor gap but the scaling mechanism transfers.

Ivie operationalizes oversight by attaching in-situ anchored explanations to just-generated code, measurably improving developer inspection. Direct mechanism for scaling human review of AI-generated code.
Empirically models how developers calibrate trust in AI code-generation tools and maps design features to support appropriate trust. Tier-1 oversight/trust-calibration hit with an actionable model.
Reproducible empirical comparison quantifying Copilot's vulnerability-introduction rates against human developers using a labeled C/C++ dataset. Direct AI-code-risk quantification with methodology.

AGDebugger provides interactive steering and error-localization mechanisms (message reset, conversation navigation) for overseeing multi-agent AI systems, a scalable-oversight pattern that transfers to agentic coding workflows. Agents aren't confirmed as coding agents, but the steering mechanism generalizes to code-review oversight without substantial re-derivation.

Empirically validated interactive task-decomposition systems for steering and verifying AI-generated analysis code, with derived design guidelines. Directly operationalizes developer verification of LLM code.

Proposes ICE-Score, a reference-free LLM-based code-quality metric correlating with functional correctness and human preference, a reusable quality signal. Org-governance framing is implicit but the scalable evaluation mechanism is concrete.

Large-scale study labeling 331k LLM-generated C programs via formal verification quantifies vulnerability rates across nine models with an explicit production risk-assessment recommendation. Strong reproducible AI-code-risk measurement.
Empirically quantifies Copilot's correctness, bug rates, and repair effort versus human solutions on algorithmic tasks. Reproducible AI-code quality measurement.

Reviews and roadmaps LLM-as-Judge frameworks as scalable surrogates for evaluating LLM-generated software artifacts, transferable scalable-oversight guidance. Largely visionary, but it consolidates operationalizable adoption paths for high-volume AI evaluation.

CodeJudge is a working LLM framework evaluating semantic correctness of generated code without test cases, outperforming prior methods. Directly usable scalable quality-assessment mechanism for AI code.

Introduces an execution-based code-hallucination taxonomy, a dynamic detection algorithm, and a benchmark across 17 LLMs, an actionable detection-and-measurement mechanism. Model-behavior focus over org process is a minor limitation but the detection tool transfers.

Presents an in-context-learning secure-generation framework plus multi-language security evaluation identifying vulnerabilities and code smells in LLM output. Actionable AI-code-risk mitigation with an explicit supply-chain risk warning.

Proposes RobustAPI benchmark and quantifies API-misuse rate (62% for GPT-4) in LLM-generated code with reproducible methodology; a directly operationalizable defect-measurement tool for orgs assessing AI code risk.

Industrial-scale LLM code-review system deployed to 12,000 WAU with reproducible precision and Outdated-Rate adoption metrics; a Tier-1 direct hit on scaling review capacity with concrete governance mechanisms.
Solar framework auto-generates test cases to quantify and mitigate social bias in AI-generated code (up to 90% reduction); a narrower but actionable risk-measurement mechanism orgs can apply.

ACCA fully automates correctness assessment of AI-generated security code via symbolic execution with strong human correlation (r=0.84); a directly operationalizable QA mechanism requiring no human intervention.
Multi-agent framework integrating static analysis and fuzzing yields a measured 13% vulnerability reduction with no functionality loss; a concrete security-hardening mechanism for AI code generation.
Reproducible experimental setup quantifies Copilot validity (91.5%) and correctness rates; usable as an org-level quality benchmark for AI-generated code.
Deployable IDE plugin operationalizes AI-augmented code review by detecting syntax/semantic issues and proposing fixes; a concrete review-augmentation mechanism on the domain anchor.
Taxonomy of vibe vs. agentic coding explicitly covering safety mechanisms, feedback loops, and human-in-the-loop oversight; sits squarely on the domain anchor with actionable governance framing.

Foundational Lee & See trust-in-automation model provides the appropriate-reliance mechanism that generalizes to trust calibration for AI-code oversight; translation to code review is trivial conceptual mapping, not domain re-derivation.
Trains DL models automating both contributor-side and reviewer-side code review with quantified success rates; directly operationalizes partial review automation.
Controlled user study (N=58) quantifies critical-security-bug rate introduced by LLM assistance with reproducible methodology; a concrete oversight-risk measurement.
SecurityEval provides a reusable CWE-mapped vulnerability benchmark (130 samples, 75 types) for evaluating code-generation security; a concrete measurement instrument.
Systematically quantifies ChatGPT code correctness, bug patterns, and maintainability issues across 4,066 programs plus a mitigation roadmap; fully operationalizable QA evidence.
SVEN controlled-code-generation method quantifiably shifts secure-code rate (59.1% to 92.3%) without harming correctness; a concrete security-hardening mechanism for LLM code.

Within-subjects study empirically shows developers struggle to understand, edit, and debug Copilot output — a direct signal on the human review burden central to scaling oversight; oversight relevance outweighs the productivity/UX framing.
Systematically characterizes six bias types in LLM-as-judge code evaluators that persist despite mitigation; a risk signal directly relevant to orgs relying on automated code review at scale.

Parasuraman & Manzey's integrated model of automation complacency and bias is a named, transferable oversight mechanism explaining reviewer over-reliance on imperfect AI aids; maps to code review with trivial translation.
CommentFinder measurably improves review-comment recommendation accuracy (+32%) and speed (49x) at scale; directly operationalizes reviewer-efficiency for high-volume review.
CodeReviewer pre-trained model with benchmark for quality estimation, comment generation, and code refinement; operationalizes review automation with reproducible evaluation.
Practitioner-validated taxonomy of 10 distinctive LLM-generated bug patterns; a concrete QA signal orgs can use to target defenses for AI code.

Introduces concrete human-in-the-loop artifacts (Merge-Readiness Packs, Consultation Request Packs) and command/execution environments for humans orchestrating agent teams at scale; this is a directly operationalizable oversight structure for governing agentic AI code. The vision-paper framing is outweighed by the specific, adoptable oversight vocabulary and process.

EvalPlus is a reproducible, open-sourced test-augmentation methodology that an org can adopt as a functional-correctness quality gate for LLM-generated code, catching wrong code that standard benchmarks miss. Direct Tier-1 hit on quantifying AI code quality with reproducible method.

Empirically studies developer trust calibration in AI code tools and designs concrete control/quality-indicator affordances for evaluating trustworthiness of suggestions. Directly serves the developer-oversight-of-LLM-code Tier-1 category.

Workshop roadmap explicitly surfaces governance, tool fragmentation, and AI-literacy pain points for GenAI in agile SE and produces short-term implementable actions. Higher-altitude than empirical papers but names actionable governance targets for engineering orgs, clearing the operationalizability bar.

Quantifies data- and model-poisoning attack efficacy against neural code autocompleters with reproducible methodology and discusses mitigations. Concrete, measurable AI-code supply-chain risk that an org must govern.

The ARC framework maps agentic risk sources (including code execution) to specific technical controls with a structured org-implementation approach. Capability-centric governance transfers directly to org-level governance of AI coding agents.

Large-scale empirical study (33k PRs) quantifying merge/rejection patterns and a taxonomy of failure modes for AI-authored pull requests. Directly operationalizable for triaging and scaling review of autonomous-agent code contributions.

Presents a decision-theoretic model for placing confirmation checkpoints in multi-step agentic tasks, validated with users. The confirmation-scheduling mechanism transfers to human oversight of AI coding agents without substantial re-derivation; task domain is generic agentic execution.

Builds a validated PLS-SEM trust/adoption model (N=238 at GitHub/Microsoft) and uses IPMA to identify concrete underperforming, high-importance design targets for developer-genAI interaction. Actionable oversight/trust-calibration contribution for engineering orgs.

Argues for adapting specific modern code-review practices (multiple anonymized reviews, checklists, defined terminology) to evaluate correctness of automatically generated patches. Directly operationalizable mechanism for reviewing machine-generated code with a trivial translation to AI code.

VulnLLMEval benchmarks LLM vulnerability detection and patching on 307 real Linux-kernel vulnerabilities with reproducible methodology, revealing LLMs struggle to distinguish vulnerable from patched code. Quantifies the risk of relying on LLMs for security tasks.

The AI-SEAL taxonomy classifies AI applications in software systems by point-of-application, technology, and automation level to support org-level risk analysis and strategy. Provides an actionable classification lens for deciding how/where to govern AI in software.

WaitGPT transforms LLM-generated code into step-by-step interactive visualizations enabling users to verify, catch errors, and steer execution. Verification mechanism transfers to human oversight of AI-generated code; the data-analysis domain is the only re-derivation needed, which is minor.

Fuzzes LLM-generated networking code and organizes discovered weaknesses into a CWE-based three-axis taxonomy, explicitly framing the risk of unreviewed AI code in firmware. Reproducible risk measurement plus direct developer-oversight framing.

VulDetectBench quantifies LLM vulnerability detection/classification/localization across five graded tasks on 17 models with a public benchmark, exposing sharp accuracy drops on detailed analysis. Reproducibly informs the limits of automated AI code security review.

Assesses three concrete visibility measures — agent identifiers, real-time monitoring, activity logging — for governing autonomous agents. Though framed for general AI agents, these mechanisms map directly onto monitoring/logging autonomous coding agents that commit and open PRs, a trivial translation rather than substantial re-derivation.

Builds ML classifiers (CNN, MCC=0.70) to automatically flag which code fragments require manual review, an action-research study with two companies. Directly addresses reviewer-workload triage/prioritization and generalizes to scaling review of AI-generated code.

Interview and Reddit study (27 professionals + 190 posts) on how developers oversee and check AI-generated code for security, yielding concrete practitioner recommendations. Direct Tier-1 hit on developer oversight and trust-checking of LLM code.

Industrial study (2,604 labeled Beko PR comments) quantifying how poorly automated LLM-as-judge evaluation aligns with human labels for code-review bots, exposing organizational-context confounds. Directly informs governance and reliability of review-scaling tools.

Position paper outlining a concrete CodeScene action-research program on LLM safeguards and trust-calibrating UI for AI refactoring, backed by large-scale repo analysis and A/B testing. Forward-looking but grounded in an operationalizable safeguard/trust mechanism for AI code change at scale.

Ethnographic case study naming four concrete factors (transparency, usability, social context, org processes) that shape engineer trust in an autonomous SE tool; directly operationalizable for org oversight of AI code tools with no re-derivation needed.

Mixed-method empirical study yielding validated guidelines for calibrating developer trust in AI code suggestions, with concrete metrics like 52% suggestion retention; squarely on the oversight/trust-calibration anchor.

Quantifies data-extraction/memorization risk in code LLMs with a reproducible benchmark and attack (47% extraction), giving orgs a measurable legal/security risk signal; weaker on oversight-scaling but passes the risk-measurement test.

Introduces comment-free reviews as a detectable proxy for LGTM (superficial) review smell with empirical impact on late commits; transfers directly to detecting rubber-stamp oversight of AI-generated changes.

Proposes a concrete data-centric auditing framework linking training-data quality to model trustworthiness indicators for stakeholder adoption decisions; vision-stage but operationalizable at the org-governance level.

Delivers a working formal-verification mechanism (Astrogator) checking LLM-generated code against a formal query language of user intent, with quantified results (83% verify, 92% detect-incorrect); a directly actionable oversight mechanism.

Provides a compliance/assurance framework (ontologies, assurance cases, factsheets) for documenting EU AI Act adherence and adversarial robustness of LLMs, which are software systems SE orgs deploy; the documentation mechanism transfers though the angle is robustness rather than code review.

Empirically identifies and quantifies GenAI-induced self-admitted technical debt from AI-referencing code comments, giving orgs a concrete detection signal for where AI-generated code accrues unreviewed risk.
Presents a validated Trust-in-Automation questionnaire, a generalizable measurement instrument that applies with trivial adaptation to measuring developer trust in AI code assistants.

Randomized experiment benchmarking reliance interventions shows they cut over-reliance but fail to improve appropriate reliance on LLM advice; the over/under-reliance findings transfer to code-suggestion oversight despite generic tasks.

Position/argument paper advocating modularity as a maintainability lens for AI-generated code but offering no measurement method, metric, or mechanism; fails the operationalizability test as descriptive-only.
Survey empirically quantifies that AI code quality degrades as problem complexity grows and points to architect-led problem decomposition as an actionable organizational mitigation.
Provides reproducible intrinsic metrics (perplexity/entropy) as a proxy for hallucination and correctness risk across languages and models, directly usable for risk triage of AI code completion.

Documents the developer-role shift toward review-heavy workload under AI code generation (more time assessing than writing), directly informing oversight capacity planning; lighter on method but on-anchor.
Gray literature review of 42 documents synthesizing practitioner challenges and proposed generative-AI solutions specifically for code review, directly relevant to oversight in the GenAI era.

SpecRover's reviewer agent produces vetted patches with an explicit confidence measure and explanations, an operationalizable trust-calibration signal for developer oversight of AI-generated fixes, evaluated at scale on SWE-Bench.
Specification-grounded LLM code-review framework deployed in industry with measured 42% developer adoption, a directly operationalizable mechanism for controllable, trustworthy AI code review.

Introduces multi-dimensional test-suite thoroughness metrics and a human-LLM collaborative test-generation method (SAGA) as a reproducible quality-detection approach; framed for RL but the verification metrics transfer to org quality assurance of AI code.
Catalogues 10 SE4FMware challenges and presents FMArts, a deployed cradle-to-grave platform for engineering trustworthy FMware, an operationalizable org-level governance framework.

Deployed ML system at Google auto-resolving code-review comments, addressing reviewer/author workload with quantified time savings (7.5% of comments, hundreds of thousands of engineer hours); directly on the review-capacity/scalability anchor.

Autonomous repo-level auditing agent with a validator module that detects confirmed real bugs at reported precision/cost/time; a directly operationalizable detection mechanism for scaling code review. Off-the-shelf actionable for engineering orgs.

Quantifies quality and security defects in ChatGPT-generated code and measures the degree of developer modification before merge with a reproducible DevGPT-based methodology. Gives orgs measurable risk signals on AI code entering repos.

Presents a representations mechanism that surfaces the AI's understanding back for user verification, with experts using it to verify and intermediates to confirm — a trust-calibration/oversight mechanism that transfers to reviewing AI-generated code. Usability framing is secondary to the transferable verification loop.

Introduces PromSec and quantifies security-vulnerability reduction while preserving functionality, with cross-language and cross-model transferability shown in reproducible experiments. A concrete measurable mitigation mechanism for insecure LLM code.

First large-scale in-the-wild study of IDE-native AI-assisted sessions showing how developers delegate diagnosis/validation and negotiate AI autonomy via context injection and behavioral constraints. Directly informs oversight practice for AI-generated code.

Systematically classifies prompting techniques and quantifies reduction of security weaknesses (notably via RCI) across GPT-3/3.5/4 on a 150-prompt dataset. Reproducible, actionable mechanism for reducing insecure AI code.

Empirically-derived Trustworthy AI framework spanning the full SDLC with actionable stakeholder guidance validated by an industrial case study and 34 practitioner interviews. Org/governance-level and software-engineering-scoped, satisfying the DevSecOps governance criterion despite general-TAI framing.

Systematic taxonomy of LM4Code pitfalls across data, design, evaluation, and deployment paired with existing solutions gives orgs an actionable risk-classification scheme for deploying code LLMs. Survey framing is offset by the concrete deployment-phase categorization.

Empirical reliability/explainability study of eight LLMs quantifying overoptimistic evaluation from data duplication and limited input-robustness. Reproducible methodology yielding measurable quality/risk signals for code generation.

Quantifies how semantically-equivalent prompt rewordings change Copilot output (~46% of cases) and affect correctness (±28%) across 892 Java methods. A reproducible risk-measurement of AI code non-robustness directly usable by engineering teams.

Interdisciplinary framework defining effectiveness conditions for human oversight (causal power, epistemic access, self-control, intentions) explicitly analyzed against EU AI Act Article 14. The oversight conditions generalize to code review, matching the Article 14 and human-oversight keep criteria.

Empirically evaluates LLM-as-judge reliability for code generation and summarization across eight LLMs on thousands of instances, exposing frequent misjudgment. Directly informs automated review mechanisms for scaling oversight.

Introduces CloudAPIBench to quantify API hallucination rates by frequency and a confidence/index-gated Documentation-Augmented-Generation mitigation with measured improvement. A concrete detection-plus-mitigation mechanism for AI code risk.

Empirical OSS study showing AI-co-authored PRs merge faster with ~80% lacking explicit review and most repos lacking AI-usage guidelines. Directly quantifies oversight erosion, review throughput, and governance gaps for AI-generated code.

Meta's diff-risk-score models operationalize risk-based gating of code changes at scale with quantified SEV-capture across logistic, BERT, and generative-LLM approaches. A directly adoptable mechanism for scaling oversight of high-volume code changes.

Provides an experimental design and proof-of-concept for studying scalable oversight where human+AI outperforms either alone. The scaling-supervision mechanism structurally parallels human review of AI code at volume, satisfying the scalable-oversight transferability criterion despite QA-task setting.

MCTS-Judge is a concrete test-time-scaling LLM-as-judge framework with unit-test-level rewards that substantially raises code-correctness evaluation accuracy at lower token cost. A quantified detection mechanism deployable in automated review pipelines.

Magentic-UI delivers concrete human-in-the-loop interaction mechanisms (co-planning, action guards, co-tasking) for overseeing agents that perform code execution and software tasks, with safety evaluation. Directly operationalizable oversight controls for agentic coding.

SecLLMHolmes is a fully automated framework quantifying LLM reliability at identifying security vulnerabilities across 228 scenarios and eight investigative dimensions, exposing non-robustness to trivial code changes. Reproducible measurement of a key AI code risk.

First evaluation quantifying deprecated-API usage rates in LLM code completion across seven models, eight libraries, and 28k prompts, plus two concrete fixing approaches. Reproducible risk measurement with actionable mitigation.

Survey delivers a unified framework and concrete research/practice directions for LLM-based vulnerability detection that orgs can use to select and structure detection tooling for their code; against it is the lack of new measurement, but the actionable framework outweighs that.

IRIS is a reproducible neuro-symbolic detection mechanism quantified against a CodeQL baseline on a validated benchmark, a directly deployable oversight signal for whole-repository code including AI-generated code; no meaningful argument against keeping.

LLMSecGuard is an operationalizable open-source framework pairing static analyzers with LLMs to harden generated code plus a model-security benchmark; directly actionable mitigation for orgs deploying AI code.

Trains critic models that catch more bugs in AI-written code than paid human reviewers, directly operationalizing scalable human oversight of LLM code; this is a Tier 1 direct hit with no serious counterargument.
Quantified head-to-head benchmark of LLMs vs static analyzers with precision/recall/effort metrics and an explicit hybrid triage-then-verify pipeline orgs can adopt; clear keep.

Line-level defect-prediction gives a concrete cost-effective risk-prioritization mechanism for scaling commit review triage under high volume; though not AI-specific, the triage mechanism transfers to reviewing AI-generated commits without re-derivation.

Explainable JIT defect-prediction bot integrated into GitHub CI/CD flags risky commits with mitigation plans, an operationalizable review-triage mechanism; the CI/CD-embedded scaling angle transfers directly to overseeing high-volume AI contributions.

Presents an accept/defer human-AI teaming optimization that weighs verification cost and individual accuracy against team utility, structurally parallel to a reviewer deciding to accept or verify an AI suggestion; the framework generalizes to code oversight with only modest translation.

Quantifies that developers overlook AI-generated programming misinformation 39% of the time and that 52% of answers are wrong, a measurable automation-bias/trust-calibration risk for AI-assisted coding; the Q&A framing is narrower than code-review workflows but the oversight-risk measurement transfers.
Reproducible scenario-based code-smell measurement benchmarking LLM output against professional reference code directly quantifies AI code-quality risk with an automated test system; clear keep.

Derives four XAI feature types and explainability needs for GenAI code tools from workshops with 43 engineers, an actionable design framework for building oversight tooling; less a governance mechanism than a design contribution but still operationalizable.
Quantifies readability, complexity, comment density, and naming across AI vs human code with reproducible metrics, a direct measurement of AI code-quality; clear keep.

SafeCoder is an operationalizable security fine-tuning technique that measurably cuts unsafe generation ~30% while preserving utility, an actionable mitigation orgs can apply when deploying code LLMs; clear keep.
Mixed-methods study identifying concrete factors driving trust and acceptance of AI code suggestions with design recommendations, a Tier 1 developer-oversight/trust-calibration hit; clear keep.
Large-scale (500k samples) reproducible comparison of AI vs human code defects and vulnerabilities using ODC and CWE classification, directly quantifying AI code risk profiles; clear keep.

Multi-agent framework generating comprehensive automated code-review comments with a review-dimension taxonomy, cost/latency analysis, and benchmarked quality, directly addressing review throughput and comprehensiveness at scale; clear keep.

VulBench benchmark quantifies LLM vulnerability-detection performance against deep-learning and static baselines, operationalizable for tool selection in a detection pipeline; evaluation-focused rather than governance-focused but actionable.

Devises an LLM-perplexity-based approach to flag code regions likely to confuse human reviewers, correlated with EEG confusion signals, a plausible oversight-prioritization signal; the mechanism is early-stage but transfers to review triage.

Large-scale empirical characterization of how agentic PRs differ from human PRs on commit, file, and description-to-diff metrics gives orgs a concrete baseline for triaging autonomous AI contributions; directly on the domain anchor.

GPTScan's LLM-plus-static-confirmation mechanism is real but its scenario/property definitions and vulnerability classes are Solidity smart-contract specific (re-entrancy, Web3 logic bugs), requiring substantial domain re-derivation to apply to general AI code review; it targets contract auditing rather than AI-generated code oversight.

Benchmarks maintainability tools (CodeScene, SonarQube, Index, ML) against human expert judgment, explicitly motivated by AI-driven code volume, and yields a validated, actionable quality-assessment method orgs can adopt. Operationalizable measurement with a concrete recommendation wins clearly.

Empirically measures adoption, review-time reduction, merge likelihood, and developer override of AI-generated PR content across 18K PRs, directly evidencing human oversight behavior over AI output. The override/complement finding is exactly the oversight practice the SLR targets.

Benchmarks generation-based code review automation and introduces the Edit Progress metric, giving a reproducible way to evaluate tools that scale review capacity. It centers on model comparison rather than org workload, but the evaluation mechanism transfers directly to assessing AI review tooling.

Mixed-methods study identifies which uncertainty-highlighting technique helps programmers catch AI code-completion errors faster and with more targeted edits, a directly adoptable oversight mechanism. Concrete, tested design guidance for scaling human review of AI code wins.

Strongest keep: proposes human-expert-in-loop and gap-analyzes GRC frameworks (NIST, COBIT, ISO 42001) at org governance level. Strongest discard, which wins: scope is general LLM commercialization/cybersecurity governance with no software-development or AI-generated-code connection, so applying it to code oversight requires substantial re-derivation.

Strongest keep: a finetuned vuln-detection model is a detection signal that could scan code. Strongest discard, which wins: it is general-purpose Java vulnerability detection with no AI-generated-code or oversight/governance framing—tool capability without the code-governance angle the SLR requires.

Execution-based agentic-coding benchmark showing SOTA agents resolve only 11% of complex feature tasks versus 74% on bug-fix benchmarks, giving orgs a concrete risk signal for where autonomous AI code needs heightened oversight. The reliability-boundary quantification is actionable governance evidence.

Strongest discard: no explicit AI angle—it targets human-reviewer turnover and workload. Strongest keep, which wins: it delivers a validated, deployable review-recommender (SofiaWL) that balances reviewer workload, expertise, and risk—the exact reviewer-capacity mechanism needed to scale oversight against AI PR volume, transferable with trivial re-derivation.

Proposes quantitative trust-in-automation models (signal detection, calibration, model-based control) that the rubric explicitly identifies as generalizing to code-review oversight. Domain-general but the trust-calibration measurement mechanism transfers to AI-code trust without re-derivation.

Quantifies LLM (GPT-4, Claude) effectiveness and failure modes on 223 real-world C/C++ vulnerability repairs, showing where AI fixes break down on cross-cutting semantics. Directly informs how much human oversight AI-assisted security fixes require.

First empirical evaluation of AIGC detectors on code content across 492K samples plus a human study, quantifying how reliably AI-generated code can be flagged. A direct detection mechanism for governance policies distinguishing AI from human code.

Enterprise case study at Google quantifying agent-based repair success (73% plausible/43% correct on machine-reported, lower on human-reported bugs) on an industrially relevant benchmark. Provides a real organizational baseline for AI code reliability and oversight need.

Introduces vulnerability-constrained decoding that measurably identifies 62% and avoids generating 67% of vulnerable auto-completed code, an actionable generation-side mitigation. Smart-contract case study, but the mechanism generalizes to reducing AI-code risk at the source.

Interpretability framework (CodeQ) plus a 37-participant study revealing AI code-generation reasoning is significantly misaligned with developers, supporting trust calibration for oversight. Mechanism is exploratory but offers an operationalizable global-explanation approach for LM4Code trust.

Preregistered two-phase controlled experiment (151 participants) measuring whether AI-co-developed code imposes downstream maintainability burden on other developers. Reproducible, org-relevant quality evidence on a core AI-code risk.

Creation-time Circuit Breaker model predicts high-maintenance agent PRs at AUC 0.96, capturing 69% of high-effort PRs at a 20% review budget—a directly deployable triage mechanism for scaling reviewer capacity against AI PR volume. Exactly the scalable-oversight contribution the SLR seeks.

Strongest keep: test-inclusion patterns in agentic PRs could serve as a review-triage signal. Strongest discard, which wins: the paper is explicitly descriptive—it reports frequencies and correlations with no proposed metric, mechanism, or governance process to act on, failing the operationalizability test.

Introduces DeepCRCEval, a reference-free evaluation framework for automated code-review-comment generation plus an LLM-Reviewer baseline, and shows text-similarity metrics fail. Directly supports scaling review-quality assessment as AI-generated review volume grows.

Presents decision-oriented programming and the Aporia probe that surfaces, tracks, and validates AI coding-agent design decisions as executable tests, with a study showing 5x better mental-model accuracy. A concrete human-oversight mechanism for AI agent decisions.

Workplace RCT plus diary study finding sustained AI-tool use raised usefulness perceptions but left trust in AI code unchanged, with practical recommendations for balancing productivity against increased scrutiny. Centers on beliefs rather than a single mechanism, but yields adoptable oversight guidance on scrutinizing AI output.

Large-scale empirical study attributes and tracks AI-introduced smells, correctness, and security issues across 300k+ commits with reproducible static-analysis methodology; directly quantifies AI code risk and maintenance debt for governance.

Reference-free code review comment quality metric grounded in static analysis and LLM-detected issues is directly operationalizable for orgs scaling automated review assessment. Provides measurement mechanism plus an annotated corpus.
White-box framework predicts LLM code correctness from internal representations, a reproducible in-process detection signal usable as a QA gate. Directly serves oversight scaling.

Introduces CodeGuard+ benchmark and metrics jointly quantifying security and correctness of LLM code, plus a constrained-decoding mitigation. Both measurement and actionable mitigation for AI code risk.

Empirical study of AGENTS.md context-file adoption across 466 OSS projects examines a real organizational governance mechanism for steering autonomous coding agents. Directly relevant to org-level control of AI code generation.

Hybrid KBS+LLM method measurably improves relevance and completeness of automated review comments, an operationalizable mechanism for scaling review capacity. Technical framing does not negate the oversight-scaling contribution.

CodeJudgeBench benchmarks LLM-as-judge reliability on coding tasks and surfaces order-sensitivity and randomness, an actionable caution and evaluation method for orgs deploying automated review at scale.

Quantifies LLMs' tendency to drop security measures during repair and proposes an RL reward mechanism to force security hardening, an operationalizable mitigation for AI code security risk. Generation-side but risk-focused.

COCO provides a reproducible robustness-testing technique that quantifies instruction-sensitivity inconsistencies in commercial generators like Copilot and ChatGPT, a concrete detection method for AI code reliability.

Benchmarks ChatGPT as a vulnerability detector on existing C/C++ code rather than addressing oversight or governance of AI-generated code; it is a tool-capability comparison off the domain anchor. Actionability is limited to a general 'fine-tune first' caution.

Empirically shows LLM evaluators capitulate to user rebuttals and casual framing, a reliability risk that transfers directly to LLM-as-judge code review where developers push back. The oversight-scaling mechanism generalizes without domain re-derivation.

Empirically measures LLM-as-judge alignment with human evaluators across code generation/translation/summarization, quantifying where automated evaluation can replace humans. Directly operationalizable for scaling review oversight.

Provides a calibration framework for code-generating models explicitly tied to graduated review-effort decisions (use, review, discard). A concrete mechanism for allocating human oversight to AI code.

Human-feedback calibration loop improves code smell detection F1 by aligning models to individual reviewer definitions, a mechanism that transfers to review triage and prioritization. Not AI-generation specific but directly adoptable.

HCAI governance framework offers 15 concrete recommendations at team/org/industry levels grounded in software engineering practices (audit trails, V&V, review boards). Operationalizable org-level governance directly applicable to AI coding tools.

Presents an ephemeral-UI scaffolding mechanism to help developers understand and guide LLM-generated code, a developer-oversight mechanism per Tier 1. Novice/tutorial context leaves scale transferability uncertain—human should verify.

Observational study of 24 professional engineers yields a trust/usefulness framework where company policy is an explicit external factor, relevant to oversight and governance context. Passes the framework test though it lacks a concrete review mechanism.

BaxBench is a reproducible benchmark quantifying both functional correctness and executable security exploitability of LLM-generated backends, directly measuring AI code risk for governance decisions.

Introduces a nine-label taxonomy and LLM classifier for code review comment quality, an operationalizable mechanism for review triage and quality control. Applicable to scaling oversight even if not framed around AI-code volume.

Refine4LLM operationalizes correctness verification of LLM code via refinement calculus and directly targets trust/opacity — a concrete oversight mechanism. Tier 1 direct hit on AI code risk with reproducible methodology.

Comparative security assessment of AI code generators yields actionable guidance on vulnerability-prone generated code; the student framing does not undercut the transferable security-measurement finding. Keep survives because the security suggestions apply to any developer/org context.
Uncertainty-estimation-with-abstention is a reproducible, execution-adjacent oversight mechanism that drives incorrect LLM code near zero. Directly actionable quality-gate for scaling review.

Reproducible benchmark of nine clone detectors against AI-generated code with concrete tool-selection and hybrid-approach guidance. Actionable detection signal for AI-code provenance/quality governance.
Empirical study of ChatGPT code persistence in GitHub projects with explicit pre-integration review implication. Measurement plus oversight recommendation clears the operationalizability test.

CodeBreaker demonstrates detection-evading backdoors in code-completion models, characterizing an AI-code attack surface orgs must defend; offensive framing is the argument against, but the risk characterization is directly actionable for defense. Tier 1 AI code security risk wins.
Quantifies security API misuse in LLM-generated Java (~70%) with reproducible task-based methodology and a misuse taxonomy. Tier 1 measurement of AI code risk.
Empirical refactoring study proposing RefactoringMirror, a concrete detect-and-reapply tactic mitigating unsafe LLM refactorings. Actionable risk-mitigation mechanism for AI-generated code.
Delivers a validated AI-generated-code detection model outperforming SOTA, a directly usable provenance/governance signal. Reproducible detection methodology.
Quantifies Copilot correctness and complexity via reproducible LeetCode+SonarQube benchmark. Tier 1 AI code quality measurement.
Safety-critical case study proposing Prompt-FDC and an adapted V-model lifecycle for governing LLM code generation. Concrete process/mechanism at engineering-org level.

Human–machine control-problem framework with six design principles and a complementary-coupling strategy for automation complacency. Tier 2: mechanism generalizes to scaling human oversight of AI code review without substantial re-derivation.
IDE-integrated tool for real-time vulnerability detection, classification, severity, and repair, validated by user study. Directly usable oversight mechanism for engineering teams.

Large-scale empirical mapping of developer AI-adoption to Responsible AI priorities (reliability, security, transparency, steerability) across SE tasks. Actionable contextual guidance for governing where AI code support is trusted.

Agentsway formalizes a governance-oriented methodology for AI-agent teams with defined roles, human orchestration, and trust/productivity metrics; argument against is that it is proposal-heavy without empirical validation. Keep because the roles-and-orchestration framework is directly adoptable as an oversight structure.

First large-scale measurement of coding-agent adoption and larger agent-assisted commits, quantifying the volume pressure that motivates scaled oversight; argument against is that it is descriptive without a mechanism. Keep because the adoption/commit measurements are actionable context for governance sizing.

Large-scale empirical security analysis of agent skills with a validated detection framework (SkillScan), grounded vulnerability taxonomy, and open toolkit. Directly actionable governance and detection contribution.

Benchmarks reasoning LLMs for vulnerability detection with quantified accuracy/F1 and prompt-design guidance; reads closer to capability comparison than org governance, but the detection framework and prompt method are usable oversight tooling. Keep on operationalizability.

ACE automates validated LLM refactoring for technical-debt remediation with correctness checks, a directly usable tool for engineering teams. Clears operationalizability via validated-output mechanism.
Repository-level benchmark quantifying security of AI-generated code with reproducible methodology; directly actionable for model selection and risk assessment. Core Tier-1 hit.

Empirically shows ChatGPT security outputs are too generic to serve as a practical oracle, giving orgs concrete guidance against unvalidated LLM reliance for security tasks. Directly operationalizable.

Provides an empirically-derived taxonomy of non-syntactic LLM code mistakes plus a detection method (GPT-4/ReAct F1 0.65) usable as a review signal; closer to error analysis than governance but still actionable.

Systematic review yielding a human-automation error-management framework (detect/understand/correct) with variables addressable by design and training; the loop maps cleanly onto reviewers catching AI-code errors without substantial re-derivation.

Systematic review categorizing causes/solutions of algorithm aversion into five actionable themes that transfer to developer trust calibration for AI code suggestions; foundational to the trust-calibration cluster in scope.

Proposes and empirically validates an explanation-generating debugging technique that measurably improved developer patch-correctness judgments; a direct, tested oversight mechanism for AI-produced code.
Quantifies LLM smart-contract audit detection and false-positive rates against a benchmark, directly informing whether manual audit oversight remains necessary at scale. Reproducible measurement.
Measures and taxonomizes LLM code-generation bug types on reproducible benchmarks and proposes a self-critique repair mechanism; both a risk measurement and an oversight mechanism.

Synthesizes a trust/transparency/vigilance framework positioning aversion vs complacency with concrete strategies (dynamic task allocation, confidence communication); mechanism transfers to code-review oversight though not software-specific.
Quantifies systematic LLM-reviewer overcorrection and proposes a concrete Fix-guided Verification Filter safeguard for automated review pipelines; direct Tier-1 hit on scaling code review.

Canonical scalable-oversight mechanism (AI critiques help humans catch flaws they would miss) with scaling analysis; task is summarization but the critique-assist loop transfers directly to code review.
Empirically measures how often LLMs warn about insecure code (12.6-40% detection), giving a concrete risk metric plus a prompting tool for safer responses. Directly operationalizable.

Provides a benchmarking methodology and a critical detection-reliability finding (ML vuln detectors can't distinguish vulnerable from patched code), an actionable caveat for orgs relying on automated detection to scale code oversight.
Foundational three-layer (dispositional/situational/learned) trust-in-automation model with design recommendations that transfer directly to developer trust calibration for AI-generated code.

Signal-detection model showing overall human-machine performance depends on tuning the automated monitor's threshold to human workload; generalizes to configuring AI code-alert sensitivity for reviewer oversight.

Parasuraman stages/levels-of-automation meta-analysis is the canonical transferable framework for allocating oversight authority between human reviewers and AI code generation, with quantified failure/awareness tradeoffs.
Empirical reliability/explainability study of code-generation LLMs exposing data-duplication-inflated results and limited input robustness; directly informs risk assessment of AI-generated code.
Finds false alarms harm performance more than misses and clarifies compliance/reliance interdependence; transfers directly to how reviewers should weight and tune AI code-review tool alerts.
Proposes an operational CMM-style maturity framework for organizations assessing and improving LLM-based development usage; explicit org/governance-level contribution.

Empirically links automation misses to reduced complacency and fewer omission errors; generalizes to managing reviewer complacency toward AI code-quality aids despite the process-control study domain.

Benchmark for evaluating LLM-judge reliability (including coding) is a transferable scalable-oversight mechanism for using LLM judges to triage AI-generated code; the objective-correctness pipeline gives orgs a validation method. Argument against (not code-governance specific) loses because the judge-reliability signal transfers directly to code triage.

Empirically quantifies code review quality against reviewer workload and experience via SZZ bug-inducing-change detection, a reproducible methodology directly operationalizable for review-capacity governance under AI code volume.

Quantifies how triage filters and augments issue volume to reduce developer load, a scaling mechanism that transfers directly to triaging high-volume AI-generated contributions before review. Lack of explicit AI angle is outweighed by the trivial translation of the filtering/throughput mechanism.

Describes a legacy rule-based code-inspection assistant as a tool capability with no measurement, metric, or org-governance finding, failing the operationalizability test. Structural analogy to human oversight of aids is too weak and predates AI code generation, so the keep argument does not hold.

Surveys automation-bias factors (trust, reliability, transparency, accountability, workload) that map directly onto human oversight of AI coding decision aids, a transferable framework requiring no domain re-derivation.

Meta-analysis of degree-of-automation performance/situation-awareness tradeoffs is the canonical Parasuraman levels-of-automation framework, transferable for allocating AI code-generation autonomy versus human oversight.
Introduces a benchmark and three fairness metrics quantifying social-bias risk across the code-generation pipeline, directly operationalizable for organizational risk assessment of LLM code output.
Benchmark quantifying human-in-the-loop value across four intervention levels in agentic coding directly measures oversight-scaling tradeoffs with a reproducible toolkit.

Empirically establishes that pre-decisional accountability increases verification and reduces automation-related errors, a transferable governance lever for structuring human oversight of AI code review.

Qualitative study codifies reviewer burden, trust erosion, and concrete mitigation strategies for AI-generated code as a tragedy of the commons, directly operationalizable for team-level oversight of AI slop.

Proposes a reproducible hybrid metric (correctness plus syntactic similarity) that better correlates with human-judged value of AI-pair-programmer output, giving orgs an evaluation mechanism beyond pass/fail.

Establishes and quantifies the automation-bias construct (omission/commission errors, accountability effects) that is foundational and explicitly transferable to human oversight of AI code aids; cockpit domain requires only trivial re-framing.

Provides an empirical trust-calibration model showing trust tracks automation reliability, directly relevant to calibrating developer trust in AI-generated code. Domain-specific fault-management framing is outweighed by the transferable reliability-trust mechanism listed under Tier 1 trust calibration.

Presents the levels-of-automation and adaptive-automation framework for allocating function between human and machine to preserve situation awareness, a transferable mechanism for scheduling AI autonomy in code review. The physical control-task setting requires only modest translation of the LOA/AA framework.

Explicitly maps the EU trustworthy-AI key requirements onto established software engineering and testing practices, giving software orgs an operational assessment framework with a direct software-system connection.

Benchmark quantifying end-to-end AI coding-agent acceptance rate and failure modes (system design, complexity, resource management) with reproducible LLM-assisted-review methodology usable for org risk assessment.

Empirical AI-driven cybersecurity framework embeds a five-level maturity model and validated best practices into the SDLC, directly operationalizable for organizational risk governance of secure coding.

Delivers a reproducible taxonomy of 12 code-hallucination types with causes, impacts, and training-free prompt-based mitigations plus a replication package — directly actionable detection and risk signals for governing LLM-generated code. Strongest counterargument (no org-level governance framing) fails because the operationalizability test is met by the measurable, transferable taxonomy and mitigation methods.

Large-scale empirical security study of AI agent skills with a validated detection framework (86.7% precision, grounded 14-pattern taxonomy) and quantified vulnerability prevalence — a Tier 1 direct hit giving orgs actionable vetting/permission mechanisms for AI-generated executable extensions. The only argument against (agent-skill focus rather than pure code review) is outweighed by its concrete detection signals and org-level vetting recommendations.
